name: py-straight-skeleton

metadata.name: py_straight_skeleton
metadata.summary: Compute the straight skeleton of a polygon and its resulting polygonal faces.

documentation:
# py_straight_skeleton

Implementation of [Straight Skeleton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Straight_skeleton) computation, with minimal
dependencies.

To the best of our knowledge, our **results compete or surpass** some of the top libraries to compute straight skeletons
of simple polygons. By "surpassing" we mean that, in our tests, our library can properly support polygons that other
libraries can not.

Please note, however, that the code is designed for readability and ease of use, and not necessarily for
runtime **performance**.

Our implementation uses a novel modification of _Felkel, Petr & Obdržálek, Štěpán (1998)_ algorithm, to address some shortcomings found during its evaluation, which failed to compute proper skeletons for several of our test cases.

Input | Result
:---------------------------------------:|:---------------------------------------:
![Polygon](docs/images/fig2_polygon.png) | ![Result](docs/images/fig3_results.png)

## Usage

```text
    📝 Note: We use (+X right, +Y up) axes as our coordinate system.
```

     ![Coordinate System](docs/images/fig1_coordinate_system.png)

```python
# Define your polygon (counter-clockwise) with optional holes (clockwise).
TEST_EXTERIOR = [[0, 0], [4, 0], [4, -2], [6, -2], [6, 0], [10, 0], [10, 10], [0, 10]]
TEST_HOLES = [[[4, 6], [6, 6], [6, 4], [4, 4]]]

from py_straight_skeleton import compute_skeleton
skeleton = compute_skeleton(exterior=TEST_EXTERIOR, holes=TEST_HOLES)
```

Check [examples/demo.ipynb](examples/demo.ipynb) for a full example with plotting and skeleton parsing.

Skeleton parsing is generally done via three methods:

```python
# 
# @property
# nodes(self) -> list[SkeletonNode]:
# 
print(skeleton.nodes)

[SKN(0, 0.000, 0.000, 0.00000), SKN(1, 4.000, 0.000, 0.00000), SKN(2, 4.000, -2.000, 0.00000), SKN(3, 6.000, -2.000, 0.00000), SKN(4, 6.000, 0.000, 0.00000), SKN(5, 10.000, 0.000, 0.00000), SKN(6, 10.000, 10.000, 0.00000), SKN(7, 0.000, 10.000, 0.00000), SKN(8, 4.000, 6.000, 0.00000), SKN(9, 6.000, 6.000, 0.00000), SKN(10, 6.000, 4.000, 0.00000), SKN(11, 4.000, 4.000, 0.00000), SKN(12, 5.000, -1.000, 1.00000), SKN(13, 5.000, 1.000, 1.00000), SKN(14, 2.000, 8.000, 2.00000), SKN(15, 8.000, 8.000, 2.00000), SKN(16, 8.000, 2.000, 2.00000), SKN(17, 2.000, 2.000, 2.00000), SKN(18, 5.000, 2.000, 2.00000)]

# 
# get_faces(self) -> list[SkeletonFace]
# 
for face_idx, face in enumerate(skeleton.get_faces()):
    nodes_pos = [skeleton.nodes[v_idx].position for v_idx in face]
    print(f"face {face_idx} -> {nodes_pos}")

face 0 -> [Vector2(0.000, 0.000), Vector2(4.000, 0.000), ...]
face 1 -> [Vector2(4.000, 0.000), Vector2(4.000, -2.000), ...]
face 2 -> [Vector2(4.000, -2.000), Vector2(6.000, -2.000), ...]
face 3 -> [Vector2(6.000, -2.000), ...]
...
face 11 -> [Vector2(4.000, 4.000), Vector2(4.000, 6.000), ...]

# 
# arc_iterator(self) -> Iterator[tuple[SkeletonNode, SkeletonNode]]
# 
for skv1, skv2 in skeleton.arc_iterator():
    print(f"{skv1.position} -> {skv2.position}")

Vector2(4.000, -2.000) -> Vector2(5.000, -1.000)
Vector2(6.000, -2.000) -> Vector2(5.000, -1.000)
Vector2(6.000, 0.000) -> Vector2(5.000, 1.000)
...
Vector2(8.000, 2.000) -> Vector2(5.000, 2.000)
Vector2(2.000, 2.000) -> Vector2(5.000, 2.000)
```

## Visualization

The [plotting module](src/py_straight_skeleton/plotting.py) provides several utilities to plot results and intermediate
steps of the algorithm for debugging and verification.

Eg:

```python
# skeleton = ...

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 4))
ax.set_aspect("equal")

from py_straight_skeleton.plotting import Utils
Utils.plot_skeleton(skeleton, ax=ax, show_vertex_info=True)

fig.savefig("output.png")
```

![Output](docs/images/fig5_output.png)

## Debugging

The [plotting module](src/py_straight_skeleton/plotting.py) also provides a `PlotTracer` that can help debug steps,
although we encourage opening issues in the repository for help if necessary.

```python
import logging
from py_straight_skeleton import compute_skeleton
from py_straight_skeleton.algorithm import set_global_algorithm_tracer
from py_straight_skeleton.plotting import PlotTracer

# Define your polygon (counter-clockwise) with optional holes (clockwise).
TEST_EXTERIOR = [[0, 0], [4, 0], [4, -2], [6, -2], [6, 0], [10, 0], [10, 10], [0, 10]]
TEST_HOLES = [[[4, 6], [6, 6], [6, 4], [4, 4]]]

# Set plot tracer to be notified by the algorithm steps and output to a folder.
plot_tracer = PlotTracer(
    fig_cs=2.5, 
    step_fig_ncols=3, 
    step_fig_nrows=3, 
    log_level=logging.INFO, 
    output_dir="examples/plot_tracer_output/",
)
set_global_algorithm_tracer(plot_tracer)

skeleton = compute_skeleton(exterior=TEST_EXTERIOR, holes=TEST_HOLES)
```

![Steps 1+](examples/plot_tracer_output/skel_plot_step_1.png)
![Steps 10+](examples/plot_tracer_output/skel_plot_step_10.png)

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.SkeletonNode

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.SkeletonNode.__init__
documentation: Initialize a skeleton node with a given id, and its position and a time.
Internal representation of vertices in the skeleton (aka nodes).

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton.__init__
documentation: Initialize the skeleton with the original edges of the polygon.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton._add_skeleton_node
documentation:
Add an internal node from the given external vertex, at the given time.

Args:
    vertex: External vertex to add to the skeleton.
    at_time: Time of the vertex, which is the distance from the original polygon edges.

Returns:
    The newly created skeleton node.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton._find_skeleton_node
documentation:
Find the skeleton node corresponding to the given external vertex, and raise an error if not found.

Args:
    vertex: External vertex to find its corresponding skeleton node.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the node is not found in the skeleton.

Returns:
    The skeleton node corresponding to the given external vertex.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton._find_or_add_skeleton_node
documentation:
Find the skeleton node corresponding to the given external vertex, or create a new one if not found.

If the node is found, the time previously stored must match the given time, otherwise an error is raised.
If the given time is 0, this method should not be called, and `_find_skeleton_node()` should be used instead,
since the original vertices are expected to be found after construction, as long as original edges are properly
specified.

Args:
    vertex: External vertex to find or add to the skeleton.
    at_time: Time of the vertex, which is the distance from the original polygon edges.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the time is zero, since you should call `_find_skeleton_node()` instead.
    RuntimeError: If the node is found but the given time does not match the stored time.

Returns:
    The skeleton node corresponding to the given external vertex.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton.add_arc
documentation:
Add an arc between the given external vertices, at the given time. Note that the time is a reference to the
time of the second vertex, and that the first vertex must already exist in the skeleton, either as an original
vertex or as a previously added vertex.

Args:
    vertex_1: External vertex to connect.
    vertex_2: External vertex to connect.
    at_time: Time at which the second vertex happens.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton._compute_faces_if_needed
documentation:
Compute the faces that the polygon is divided into by the skeleton, and store them in the instance.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the faces could not be computed, for example if the skeleton is not a valid skeleton graph.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton.nodes
documentation: Get all the nodes in the skeleton, including both polygon-original vertices and additional nodes.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton.get_faces
documentation:
Get the faces that the polygon is divided into by the skeleton, as a list of internal node ids. Note that
faces will be computed if they have not been cached yet.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.skeleton.Skeleton.arc_iterator
documentation:
Iterate the arcs in the skeleton, as pairs of nodes that are connected by an arc. Note that this iterator
iterates the arcs directionally only once, despite the arcs not having a real direction.

Straight skeleton of a polygon. The skeleton is a graph of nodes and the arcs that connect them. Additionally,
this class provides the time per node (=the distance from the original polygon edges), and computation of faces,
so that the polygon can be triangulated and lifted.

modules.name: py_straight_skeleton.constants
documentation: Module with constant definitions to share across code.

functions.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.set_global_polygon_math_tracer
documentation: Set a custom global tracer for polygon math operations.

functions.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.is_vector_in_segment_polygon
documentation:
Check if a candidate vector is inside the polygon that the two segments belong to. The polygon, by convention,
is to the left of the segments. Note that here "polygon" may refer to the polygon defined by a LAV, not necessarily
the initial polygon.

Args:
    prev_segment: Segment that arrives to the vertex in the polygon we are checking.
    next_segment: Segment that leaves the vertex in the polygon we are checking.
    candidate_vector: Vector whose direction we want to check, and that is considered as starting on the same
        vertex as the segments.
    policy: Policy to follow when comparing against parallel segments. See InsidePolygonCheckPolicy.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the policy is set to RAISE_ERROR and the segments are (parallel) aligned or opposite.
    ValueError: If the specified policy is not recognized.

Returns:
    True if the candidate vector is inside the left polygon defined by the two given segments, False otherwise.

functions.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.is_point_inside_polygon_edges
documentation:
Determine if a point is inside a polygon defined by its edges using the ray-casting algorithm.

Note holes are purposely ignored, as they are not relevant for this check. This method returns true even if
the point is inside a hole.

Args:
    edges: List of edges that form the outside of the polygon. Holes are not relevant for this check.
    point2d: Point to check if it is inside the polygon.

Returns:
    True if the point is inside the polygon exterior, defined by its edges, False otherwise.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.PolygonMathTracerProtocol

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.PolygonMathTracerProtocol.on_compute_from_edges_are_opposite
documentation: Called when the edges that spawn a new vertex are parallel and face opposite directions (anti-parallel).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.PolygonMathTracerProtocol.on_compute_from_edges_are_aligned
documentation: Called when the edges that spawn a new vertex are parallel and face the same direction (aligned).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.PolygonMathTracerProtocol.on_lav_infinite_loop_detected
documentation: Called when we iterate a LAV with an infinite loop.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.PolygonMathTracerProtocol.on_degenerate_lav_detected
documentation: Called when a LAV is detected to be degenerate (all vertices are the same).
Protocol to receive notifications of key polygon math operations, specially edge cases.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.NoopPolygonMathTracer
documentation: Implementation of the PolygonMathTracerProtocol that does nothing on each notification.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.InsidePolygonCheckPolicy

class_variables.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.InsidePolygonCheckPolicy.RELAXED
documentation: Checks will generally accept cases if any condition passes.

class_variables.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.InsidePolygonCheckPolicy.STRICT
documentation: Checks will generally accept cases if all conditions pass.

class_variables.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.InsidePolygonCheckPolicy.RAISE_ERROR
documentation: Checks will raise an error, failing execution, for parallel cases (mostly for debugging).
Policy to follow when we check if a vector is inside a LAV, specifically around parallel segments.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.DegenerateLAVError
documentation: Custom exception for degenerate LAVs (all points in the LAV are the same).

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexNotComputedError
documentation: Custom exception when we try to access properties of a vertex that were not yet computed.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.__init__
documentation:
Initializes a new VertexInfo at the given position, with a pointer to its containing LAV.

Many of the attributes accessed through properties require computation, and are not available until the
computation is executed. See `compute_from_edges`.

Args:
    position: Position of the vertex.
    lav_ptr: Pointer to the LAV that contains this vertex.
    split_dummy_vertex: Optional dummy vertex that vertices created by split events may have. This is used to
        link arcs to the same vertex (the dummy vertex), even when the arcs reference one of the split vertices.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.__hash__
documentation:
For comparison. Note that only the id is taken into account, so two vertex infos at the same location are
considered different.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.next_vertex
documentation: Returns the next vertex in the LAV.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.prev_vertex
documentation: Returns the previous vertex in the LAV.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.pending_split_events
documentation: Returns the pending split events for this vertex.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.pending_split_events
documentation: Sets the pending split events for this vertex, flagging as computed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.pending_split_events_computed
documentation: Returns whether the pending split events have been computed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.is_reflex
documentation: Returns whether the vertex is reflex or not. The vertex info must be computed before accessing this property.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.force_reflex
documentation:
This allows setting the reflex property after it has been auto-computed.

Although not ideal, turns out to be necessary for edge events, which can accidentally detect reflex vertices
when they are not. We should fix that underlying issue, but for now this is a workaround.
TODO I think `force_reflex` can be removed. See note in the caller.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.bisector_direction
documentation: Returns the direction of the bisector of the vertex. The vertex info must be computed before accessing this property.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.bisector_ray
documentation: Returns a Ray2 starting at the vertex and pointing in the same direction as the vertex's bisector.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.get_prev_vertex_safe
documentation:
Iterate our linked vertices backwards until we find one that is not overlapping with us.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If an infinite loop is detected.
    DegenerateLAVError: If all vertices are overlapping, and we can't find a safe previous vertex.

Returns:
    The first vertex that is not overlapping with the vertex, found iterating backwards the linked list / LAV.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.get_next_vertex_safe
documentation:
Iterate our linked vertices forward until we find one that is not overlapping with us.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If an infinite loop is detected.
    DegenerateLAVError: If all vertices are overlapping, and we can't find a safe next vertex.

Returns:
    The first vertex that is not overlapping with the vertex, found iterating forward the linked list / LAV.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo._is_self_bisector_inside_lav
documentation:
Return whether our computed bisector is pointing towards the inside the LAV, comparing our previous
and next safe sements (that is, the first ones whose other point does not overlap with this vertex).

Returns:
    True if the bisector is pointing towards inside the LAV, False otherwise.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.VertexInfo.compute_from_edges
documentation:
Compute the bisector of the vertex and whether it is reflex, based on the edges that spawn it.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the vertex's properties were already computed.
Extended information about a Vertex in a LAV.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.__init__
documentation:
Initializes a new Edge between two vertices. The edge is automatically set on the vertices, if this
edge is an original edge. Direction of the edge is from start to end.

Args:
    start_vertex: Start vertex of the edge.
    end_vertex: End vertex of the edge.
    is_original_edge: Whether this is an edge in the original polygon.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.has_endpoint
documentation: Return whether the edge has the given vertex as one of its endpoints.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.start_p
documentation: Return the start vertex's position.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.end_p
documentation: Return the end vertex's position.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.center_p
documentation: Return the center point of the edge.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.distance_point_to_edge_line
documentation:
Return the distance from the point to the infinite line holding this edge, optionally signed/unsigned.

Args:
    point: Point to compute the distance to the edge.
    signed: Whether to return the signed distance or the absolute distance.

Returns:
    The distance from the point to the infinite line holding this edge.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.Edge.is_point_in_edge_region
documentation:
Return whether the given point is inside the region defined by this edge and the bisectors of its
vertices. The region is to the left of the edge, and the bisectors are considered as rays starting at the
vertices.

Note on `allow_inclusive`: Conceptually the checks should be inclusive, however some skeletons may fail
depending on the collinearity epsilon values if inclusivity is enabled (specifically with eps > 1 deg).

Args:
    point: Point to check if it is inside the region defined by this edge and the bisectors of its vertices.
    allow_inclusive: Whether to consider points on the boundaries as inside the region.

Returns:
    True if the point is inside the region, False otherwise
Information about an edge in the original polygon.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the LAV with an optional head vertex.

Args:
    head: Initial vertex of the LAV (if any).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV.__hash__
documentation: For storage in the set of LAVs. Note that only the id is taken into account.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV.__eq__
documentation: Compares two LAVS, based exclusively on their unique ids, regardless of other values.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV.__iter__
documentation: Iterate over the vertices in the LAV, checking for (infinite) loops.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.polygon_math.LAV.append_vertex_at
documentation:
Creates a new vertex info and adds it to the tail of the LAV, at the specified point.

Args:
    point: 2D point to add a vertex at. The other vertex properties are not yet computed, since they require
        additional information.
List of active vertices.

modules.name: py_straight_skeleton
documentation: read version from installed package

functions.name: py_straight_skeleton.compute_skeleton
documentation:
Computes the straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the given exterior outline and interior holes.

Args:
    exterior: List of vertices that define the exterior of the polygon. Must be provided in COUNTER-CLOCKWISE,
        with +X-right and +Y-up.
    holes: List of holes, each as a list of vertices that define the hole. Must be provided in CLOCKWISE order,
        with +X-right and +Y-up (opposite of the exterior vertices).

Returns:
    The straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the exterior and holes.

functions.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.set_global_algorithm_tracer
documentation: Set a custom global algorithm tracer for algorithm notifications.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_reflex_vertex_found_after_edge_event
documentation: Called when we decide a vertex is reflex after an edge event, which should not happen and causes issues.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_failed_to_find_opposite_edge
documentation:
Called when we fail to find the opposite edge for a split event, which sometimes is expected if the
opposite edge has collapsed due to other events.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_step_begin
documentation: Called at the beginning of each iteration of the algorithm's main loop.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_event_popped
documentation: Called when an event is popped from the priority queue and is about to be processed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_vertex_attempting_event_requeue
documentation: Called when a vertex that failed to process an event is attempting to requeue a new event.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_vertex_became_its_opposite_edge
documentation: Called when a vertex becomes its own opposite edge, which should not happen and can cause issues.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_algorithm_end
documentation: Called when the algorithm finishes processing all events and the skeleton is complete.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_algorith_raised_exception
documentation: Called when the algorithm raises an exception, which should not happen and signals a bug.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.AlgorithmTracerProtocol.on_debug_hit
documentation: General hook for debugging during edge cases.
Protocol to receive notifications from key algorithm steps and edge cases.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.NoopAlgorithmTracer
documentation: Implementation of the AlgorithmTracerProtocol that does nothing on each notification.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.__init__
documentation: Initialize a new empty Priority Queue.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.__len__
documentation: Return the number of items in the priority queue.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.__iter__
documentation: Iterate over the elements in priority order without modifying the queue, using a sorted copy.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.put
documentation:
Insert the given item in the queue with the given priority.

Args:
    priority: The priority of the item. Lower values are better priorities.
    item: The item to insert in the queue.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.get
documentation: Remove and return the item with the lowest(=best) priority from the queue. Raises IndexError if empty.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.peek
documentation: Return the item with the lowest(=best) priority without removing it. Raises IndexError if empty.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.PriorityQueue.empty
documentation: Return True if the priority queue is empty, False otherwise.
Priority queue implementation using a heap for efficient insertion and removal of items with priorities.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EventInfo

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EventInfo.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the event with the given type, position and distance. A new unique id is automatically assigned
to the event.

Args:
    event_type: Type of event.
    position: Point where the event happens (generally a collision point between wavefronts).
    distance: Distance to the vertex that detected the event.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EventInfo.__lt__
documentation:
Compare events based on their distance first. If the distance is the same (within epsilon), compare the auto
id of the events, thus prioritizing the event that was detected first.

class_variables.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EventInfo.EventType.EDGE
documentation: An edge collapses.

class_variables.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EventInfo.EventType.SPLIT
documentation: A reflex vertex splits an edge.
Information about an 'event' in the context of edge wavefront events.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EdgeEventInfo

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.EdgeEventInfo.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the edge event with the given intersection position, the distance to the vertex that detected the
event, and the two vertices whose bisectors cause the intersection, and therefore collapse of edge.
Information about an edge event, where two bisectors intersect to collapse an edge.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.SplitEventInfo

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.SplitEventInfo.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the split event with the given split position, the distance to the vertex causing the split, such
vertex, and the original edge that will be split.
Information about a split event, where a reflex vertex splits an edge.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the algorithm with the given number of decimal places for event priorities.

Args:
    event_time_decimals: The number of decimal places to consider to round event priorities and distances. If
        None, full precision is used. If set, the priorities and the distance to vertice will be rounded to the
        given number of decimals before they are compared. Note that if the number of decimals is less than the
        time comparison epsilon, rounding can cause issues, since rounding can pull close times apart more than
        that epsilon (eg: if decimals is 1, two close events 0.47 and 0.51 would now be 0.1 apart, which is much
        greater than the original 0.04 difference). This value should probably be >= decimals(TIME_EPSILON) + 1.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._round
documentation: Round and return the given value to the number of decimals set for the algorithm.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._compute_closest_edge_event
documentation:
Computes the edge event which is closest to the given vertex, if any. Edge events happen when the bisector
of the given vertex and the bisector of one of its neighbors intersect, collapsing the edge between them.

Args:
    vi: The vertex for which to compute the nearest edge event.

Returns:
    A tuple with the priority of the event and the event information, if an event is found. If no event is
    found, the tuple (None, None) is returned. The priority is the distance from the intersection point to the
    vanishing edge's holding line.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._compute_split_events
documentation:
Compute the split events for a given vertex, and cache them in the vertex. Split events happen when a reflex
vertex splits an opposing edge, potentially splitting the polygon into separate subpolygons.

Args:
    vi: The vertex for which to compute the nearest split event.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: In case of unexpected computations/conditions (might signal a bug).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._attempt_requeue_vertex_events
documentation:
Attempts to queue a new vertex event for the given vertex, which must be not yet processed.

This is a modification to the original algorithm, which would only consider the closest event. When events fail
(because part the other vertex has been processed or their opposite edge has collapsed), the unprocessed vetex
can be left dangling. Those vertices have now a second chance of computing their next event. In order to do so,
we compute the newest edge event possible, and check it against the next split event cached (if any).

Args:
    vi: Vertex for which to attempt to queue a new event, since their intended event failed.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If the vertex is already processed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._queue_vertex_events
documentation:
Compute the closest edge and all split events for the given vertex, and queue them with the appropriate
priority, storing the cache of split events for later use if needed. If the cache was already computed, it will
not be recomputed on subsequent calls.

Args:
    vi: Vertex for which to compute the closest events.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._initialize_from
documentation:
Initialize the algorithm's variables to compute the skeleton of a polygon defined by the given exterior
and holes.

Args:
    exterior: List of points defining the exterior of the polygon in COUNTER-CLOCKWISE orientation.
    holes: List of holes, each defined by a list of points in CLOCKWISE orientation.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._process_edge_event
documentation:
Process an edge event, potentially adding arcs to the skeleton, and queueing new events if needed.

Args:
    event_info: Event information.
    current_time: Current time.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If unexpected conditions are met (might signal a bug).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._process_split_event
documentation:
Process a split event, potentially adding an arc to the skeleton, and queueing new events if needed.

Args:
    event_info: Event information.
    current_time: Current time.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: In case of unexpected conditions (might signal a bug).

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm._compute_skeleton_impl
documentation:
Computes the straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the given exterior outline and interior holes.

Args:
    exterior: List of vertices that define the exterior of the polygon. Must be provided in COUNTER-CLOCKWISE,
        with +X-right and +Y-up.
    holes: List of holes, each as a list of vertices that define the hole. Must be provided in CLOCKWISE order,
        with +X-right and +Y-up (opposite of the exterior vertices).

Returns:
    The straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the exterior and holes.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.algorithm.StraightSkeletonAlgorithm.compute_skeleton
documentation:
Computes the straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the given exterior outline and interior holes.

Args:
    exterior: List of vertices that define the exterior of the polygon. Must be provided in COUNTER-CLOCKWISE,
        with +X-right and +Y-up.
    holes: List of holes, each as a list of vertices that define the hole. Must be provided in CLOCKWISE order,
        with +X-right and +Y-up (opposite of the exterior vertices).

Returns:
    The straight skeleton of the polygon defined by the exterior and holes.
Encapsulates the straight skeleton computation algorithm itself.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Vector2

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Vector2.normalized
documentation: Returns a copy of this vector, normalized by its length. It does not normalize itself.
2D vector/point class.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_left_exclusive
documentation: The orientation signifies left, but does not include collinear cases.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_left_inclusive
documentation: The orientation signifies left, and includes collinear cases if allowed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_right_exclusive
documentation: The orientation signifies right, but does not include collinear cases.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_right_inclusive
documentation: The orientation signifies right, and includes collinear cases if allowed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_aligned
documentation: The vectors are aligned, i.e. they are parallel and point in the same direction.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_opposite
documentation: The vectors are opposite, i.e. they are (anti)-parallel pointing in opposite directions.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.is_collinear
documentation: The vectors are collinear, i.e. they are parallel/aligned or anti-parallel/opposite.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Orientation.of_vec_with_respect_to
documentation:
Computes and returns the orientation of `vec` with respect to `wrt`. Both vectors are normalized before
computing the orientation, to guarantee that the result is consistent.

Args:
    vec: Vector to compute orientation of.
    wrt: Vector to compute orientation with respect to.
    collinearity_epsilon_degrees: Epsilon value in degrees to consider vectors collinear. If the angle between
        the vectors is less than this value, they are considered collinear.

Returns:
    _description_

Orientation of a vector with respect to another vector. Note that here we treat vectors as direction vectors. The
same orientation may not hold if the vectors are translated to their respective origins, for example when comparing
rays.

    Note: It is encouraged to use methods for comparisons instead of directly using == or != operators.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Ray2

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Ray2.__init__
documentation: Initialize the ray, automatically normalizing the direction vector.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Ray2.intersection_with_ray
documentation:
Compute intersection point of this ray with another ray.

Args:
    other: Ray to compute intersection with.
    allow_forward_collinear: Whether to allow finding an intersection point when the rays are facing the same
        direction, and the other ray's origin is ahead of this ray's origin. This is useful for bisectors to
        find intersections with other bisectors that share direction but whose vertex is ahead of ours.
    allow_backward_collinear: Whether to allow finding an intersection point when the rays are facing the same
        direction, and this ray's origin is ahead of the other ray's origin. This is useful for bisectors to
        find that their vertex is ahead and intersected by the other bisector.
    dist_epsilon: Epsilon to compare distances.
    collinearity_epsilon_deg: Epsilon in degrees to consider vectors collinear. If the angle between the vectors
        is less than this value, they are considered collinear.

Raises:
    RuntimeError: If computations take unexpected values (this would signal an implementation error).

Returns:
    The intersection point if it exists, according to the args, or None if the rays do not intersect.
2D ray class, defined by an origin and a direction vector.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Line2

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Line2.intersection_with_ray
documentation:
Compute intersection point of this line with a ray.

Args:
    ray: Ray to compute intersection with.
    collinearity_epsilon_deg: TBD

Returns:
    The intersection point if it exists, or None if the line and ray do not intersect.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Line2.intersection_with_line
documentation:
Compute intersection point of this line with another line.

Args:
    line: Line to compute intersection with.
    collinearity_epsilon_deg: TBD

Returns:
    The intersection point if it exists, or None if the lines do not intersect.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Line2.signed_distance
documentation:
Compute the signed distance of a point to this line. The sign indicates on which side of the line the point
lies.

Args:
    point: Point to compute distance to.

Returns:
    The signed distance of the point to the line.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.vector_math.Line2.find_point_with_min_distance_along_vector
documentation:
Find a point along the given vector that is at a minimum distance from this line. In order to travel the
vector we start at the given `point`, which is expected to be on the line.

Args:
    point: Point to start from. Expected to be on the line.
    vector: Vector to travel along.
    min_distance: Minimum distance that the new point should have with respect to the line. For clarity,
        this is not the same as the distance traveled along the vector.
    collinearity_epsilon_deg: Collinearity epsilon in degrees, to make sure the vector and the line
        are not collinear. If their angle is less than this value, a ValueError is raised.

Raises:
    ValueError: If the vector and the line are collinear, according to the given epsilon.

Returns:
    A tuple with the distance traveled along the vector, and the new point at that distance.
2D line class, defined by a point and a direction vector. Unlike a Ray, Lines are infinite and undirected.

modules.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting
documentation: Utilities and tracers to plot the state of the straight skeleton algorithm to Matplotlib figures.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils.plot_vertex_info
documentation:
Plot the given vertex information.

Args:
    vi: Vertex information.
    ax: Matplotlib axes.
    bisector_color: Color of the bisector. Bisector will not be shown if None.
    bisector_length: Length of the bisector.
    arrow_color: Color of the arrows that point towards the vertex's connected edges. Arrows will not be shown
        if None.
    hide_original_vertex_arrows: Whether to hide the arrows that point towards the vertex's connected edges if
        the vertex is defined by both its edges, as is the case of the original vertices.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils.plot_lav
documentation:
Plots a LAV.

Args:
    lav: Lav to plot.
    ax: Matplotlib axes.
    show_bisectors: Whether to show the bisectors of the vertices in the LAV.
    bisector_length: Length of the vertex bisectors. Ignored if `show_bisectors` is False.
    show_edge_arrows: Whether to show the arrows that point towards the vertex's connected edges.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils.plot_edges
documentation: Plots the given edges to the given Matplotlib axes, passing through the kwargs.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils.plot_queue
documentation:
Plots each event in the given queue.

Args:
    queue: Queue to plot.
    ax: Matplotlib axes.
    original_edges: Original edges of the polygon. If provided, events outside the polygon will not be plotted.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.Utils.plot_skeleton
documentation:
Plots the given skeleton to the given Matplotlib axes

Args:
    skeleton: Skeleton to plot.
    ax: Matplotlib axes to plot to.
    color: Color of the skeleton arcs.
    circle_color: Color of the circles to render at each vertex. Circles no rendered if set to None.
    show_vertex_info: If set to true, the information (id and time) of each vertex will be displayed.
    vertex_info_fontsize: Font size of the vertex information. Ignored if `show_vertex_info` is False.
    face_colors: Color or colors to render the faces of the skeleton. If a single color is provided, all faces
        will be rendered with that color. If a list of colors is provided, the faces will be rendered with the
        colors in the list, repeating if necessary. If None, faces will not be rendered. Note that this will
        cause face computation if the skeleton does not have faces computed yet.
Utilities for plotting elements of the straight skeleton algorithm.

classes.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.__init__
documentation:
Initialize the tracer with custom Matplotlib figure settings. The settings for figures are applied to
step plotting, but some may not affect other plots, such as final plots on algorithm end.

Args:
    fig_cs: Width=Height of the cells in inches. See figsize in plt.subplots.
    step_fig_nrows: Number of rows per figure before creating another for step plots.
    step_fig_ncols: Number of columns per figure before creating another for step plots.
    step_max_figs: Maximum number of figures to create for step plots. If 0 (or negative), no figures will be
        created.
    output_dir: If set, figures will also be saved to files automatically.
    log_level: Logging level to use for the tracer. It is applied to the module's logger.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer._create_new_step_figure_lazy
documentation:
Create a new Matplotlib figure if the next axis index is out of bounds, and we have not reached the limit.

Args:
    fig_name: Name of the figure to create (if one is created), to identify it when we save to disk.

Returns:
    True if we can use the next axis, False if we have reached the limit of figures and we did not create
    a new one.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer._get_next_ax
documentation:
Return a new axis to use, increasing the index for the next one and creating a new figure if necessary.

Args:
    fig_name: Name of the created figure (if one is created), to identify it when we save to disk.

Returns:
    The axis to plot to, or None if we have reached the maximum number of figures allowed.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer._save_current_figure_if_needed
documentation:
Save to disk the current figure if we have a folder set up, and a figure to save.

Args:
    close_it: Whether to close the figure after saving it. In general this should be True, but sometimes
        we may want to save a figure due to a debug event, while keeping it open for more plots.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer._plot_common_to_ax
documentation:
Plot most common elements of the algorithm to the given axis.

Args:
    ax: Axis to plot to.
    algorithm: Instance with the current state of the algorithm.
    bisector_length: Length of the vertex bisectors. If None, a default length is computed, based on a factor
        with respect to the axis size.
    show_edge_ids: Whether to show the edge ids.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_step_begin
documentation: Logs the current queue and plots the state of the algorithm if we have axes/plots left to plot to.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_event_popped
documentation: Logs the event information.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_algorithm_end
documentation: Closes the current figure, and plots the final result of the algorithm, polygonizing the skeleton.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_lav_infinite_loop_detected
documentation: Save the last plot we were able to get, since we are crashing.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_debug_hit
documentation: General function to plot the current state of the algorithm when a debug hit is triggered.

methods.name: py_straight_skeleton.plotting.PlotTracer.on_algorith_raised_exception
documentation: Save the last plot we were able to get, since we are crashing.
Tracer that plots the state of the algorithm and other events to Matplotlib figures.
